text: integrated and kept at bay in social rituals. With the transition to empire (traditionally identified with Caesar’s appointment in 44 bc), Rome speeded up its military expansion, or entered what Voegelin (1974) called the ‘ecumenic age’. But here the kinesis, the spiralling logic identified by Polybius and taken up by Voegelin as a key term to identify the moving forces of the entire ecumenic age starts to take an inward aspect as well, reflected in the term invoked by Voegelin, ‘concupiscental expansion’ (1974: 313). This development was very much related to uncontrolled play. The unsettling force in the psychological sense was one of a craving for entertainment, the titillation of the senses, in combinations of liquor, violence and sex. Outward expansionism and internal decay went hand-in-hand in a negative dialectics, pin-pointed with precision by Lewis Mumford in The City in History, as the ‘exploitation of distant territories removes the pressure to achieve balance and harmony nearer at hand’ (Mumford 1961: 242). Mumford sees with clarity how the history of Rome, ‘presents a series of classic danger signals to warn one when life is moving in the wrong direction’ (ibid.). The round-shaped arena here came to play a role which is quite similar to today’s television: The arena, particularly its gladiatorial extravaganzas, combined the contest of sports with the thrill of vicarious violence, even more efficiently than modern TV. But both the arena and the TV perverted the original urban contribution of empathy and mutual understanding into a deadening inability to live except through the lives of others. And where life itself had been most brutalized, the most exciting vicarious thrill was imagining oneself as the mutilator or murderer of others. (Reilly 1980: 85) Liminality and the Modern 154 What we witness here is something very akin to Girard’s sacrificial mechanism imploding within the centre of the world’s then most powerful empire. If one accepts Plato’s view that Athens was morally and politically undermined by sophistry, one might suggest that in Rome this loss of balance was equally connected to the ‘playful’, but also differently so. The play element evidently became incorporated into the spatial design of imperial Rome, where a growing number of public spaces turned into play stages. The most important and most visited public spaces were baths (where erotic games also thrived), arenas and theatres – all scenes of play. Mumford famously wrote that Rome, as an imperial city, became a ‘show-place for power’, and that is true of course, but it went hand- in-hand with the city turning into a show place for play and entertainment. Games became an obsession. This eventually turned into an obsession with blood and death. The Romans were much less interested in Greek-style combat sports where nobody was hurt (it was tried out on the Navona square, with no success at all). Increasingly, blood had to be shed. In the first gladiatorial games in 264 prisoners were publicly executed; but soon this was not enough. Here the parallels to the Aztec empire are striking: in the beginning political prisoners were sacrificed; but at a certain point the relationship is inverted: wars and raids were ventured because prisoners for public execution were needed. The mass killings went on and on, and the style and manner of execution became more and more sophisticated, in order to please the craving audience. Today this public and continuous display of killing and violence has of course been taken over by television and video; we don’t even react anymore when we see people killed in film; it is even funny, but also a bit boring, unless of course a move to some new kind of extreme of violence or evilness, or ‘artistic’ and creative ways of killing with smart weapons, or inflicting new degrees of physical or psychological suffering on the innocent victim. In Rome the development even started to affect the city’s entire economy, because the days on which ludi were held were public holidays, where no business could be conducted. That practically meant that an increasing number of days per year were, by law, dedicated to play and entertainment. The term ‘entertainment industry’ (revealing and frightening as the term is: the machine-like, mimetic production of mass humour) may be modern, but the fact to which it refers less so. The numbers speak for themselves: By the late Republic, ludi were held on 57 days of the year (Bunson 1995: 246); during the reign of Claudius ( ad31–54) there were 93 days of games a year at public expense. By ad 354 the number had risen to 175 such days. Half the year, half their life, Roman citizens spent the day playing, or watching play. By then there were enough arenas and theatres to hold half the population of Rome simultaneously. The social texture of the city and the empire had fatally weakened. Mumford writes that long before its fall, Rome had turned into a necropolis: a city of the living dead. The proliferation of play and games led to what was recognized by an increasing number of people as moral decay, intimately tied to the decline of the entire Roman empire, destroying itself from the inside. There was resistance to this Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 155 development, then as well. At several points in time there was such a craving for games that even emperors who wanted to downsize the phenomenon dared not do so for fear of popular rebellion (this was the case for Hadrian). Games had turned into an obsession. Rome slowly but surely turned into permanent play, and the remarkable thing is that no-one was in charge of this: the development continued as an eroding, de-civilizing force unfolding at the heart of the empire. Also then of course, the cynics and the populists stood ready to defend the derailing: if this is what people want, let them have it. And, it was added: this is good business.
